critic_raw:
The hypothesis claims at least 15% improvement in churn prediction accuracy by adding sentiment features, but accuracy is an inappropriate primary metric for churn prediction at a subscription service. Churn is typically a low-frequency event (5–20% monthly cancellation rates). At a 90% retention rate, a model predicting 'no churn' for all users achieves 90% accuracy — the claimed 15% relative improvement over a model with 90% base accuracy would require accuracy to reach 103.5%, which is impossible. The stated threshold of 15% improvement has no specified baseline value to make it interpretable. AUROC is listed as the actual evaluation metric in the methodology, contradicting the hypothesis's 'accuracy' claim — this inconsistency means the quantitative threshold in the hypothesis cannot be evaluated.

Sentiment features from customer feedback are extracted via TF-IDF and sentiment polarity on unstructured reviews. Not all customers submit reviews — the fraction of users who provide feedback is likely small and non-random (reviewers are disproportionately churned or very satisfied users). If sentiment features are missing for the majority of users, either they must be imputed (introducing noise and assumptions) or the comparison is restricted to the review-submitting population — a self-selected subset that is not representative of the full churn prediction task. This selection bias is not addressed.

A stratified random split of 3-year subscription data shuffles users across all time periods. Subscription churn is known to follow seasonal patterns (holiday cancellations, New Year resolutions, post-trial-period peaks) and is affected by pricing changes, content library updates, and competitive launches. A model trained on a randomly-mixed 3-year sample of these events will learn seasonal patterns that are present in both train and test, producing an optimistic estimate of forward-looking prediction performance.

Gradient boosting handles missing values natively, but TF-IDF features from absent reviews are structural zeros — the model may learn to predict churn from TF-IDF = 0 (i.e., absence of review submission), which is a different signal than sentiment content. This conflates 'user did not review' with 'user has neutral sentiment,' and the model may be learning review submission propensity as a churn proxy.

The logistic regression baseline uses only 'transactional data,' while the gradient boosting model uses both transactional and sentiment features. The comparison does not isolate the contribution of sentiment features from the contribution of switching to gradient boosting from logistic regression. A proper ablation would require a gradient boosting model with only transactional features as the baseline.
all_issues_raised: The hypothesis specifies '15% improvement in accuracy' but the methodology uses AUROC as the primary metric — this is a direct internal contradiction making the quantitative threshold in the hypothesis unverifiable as stated.

Sentiment features are missing for the majority of non-reviewing users; this selection bias (reviewers skew toward churned or highly satisfied users) is not addressed, and the comparison may be restricted to an unrepresentative self-selected subset.

Gradient boosting with TF-IDF features may learn 'user did not submit a review' (TF-IDF vector of zeros) as a churn proxy rather than sentiment content, conflating review submission propensity with sentiment signal.

The comparison confounds two changes simultaneously — addition of sentiment features and change from logistic regression to gradient boosting — so any observed improvement cannot be attributed solely to sentiment features without a gradient boosting baseline using only transactional data.

Seasonal and event-driven churn patterns (holiday cancellations, pricing changes) are present in all three years of data; random splitting mixes these patterns across train and test, producing optimistic estimates that overstate forward-looking performance.

Gradient boosting with TF-IDF features may learn 'user did not submit a review' (TF-IDF vector of zeros) as a churn proxy rather than review sentiment content, conflating review submission propensity with sentiment signal.

The comparison confounds two changes simultaneously — addition of sentiment features and change from logistic regression to gradient boosting — so any observed improvement cannot be attributed solely to sentiment features without a gradient boosting baseline using only transactional data.
